part.text:
Let me start by understanding the current state of the CI workflows and the project structure. The user wants me to:
1. Make CI target support declarative (manifests)
2. Generate checked-in workflow from manifests
3. Enforce that it stays in sync
4. Preserve current CI behavior unless clearly duplicated
5. Keep normal contributor workflow simple
6. Test the generator
7. Commit the finished change

Let me explore the repository structure first.
I'll explore the CI workflows and project structure to understand the current state before planning.

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: read

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}

jobs:
  hygiene:
    name: hygiene
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 30

display.text:
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: read

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}

jobs:
  hygiene:
    name: hygiene
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 30
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - run: mise run ci:lint

  docs:
    name: docs
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 60
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - run: mise run //docs:build

  variants:
    name: variant / ${{ matrix.mise_env }}
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.runner }}
    timeout-minutes: 120
    env:
      MISE_ENV: ${{ matrix.mise_env }}
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - mise_env: linux-arm64-egl
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
          - mise_env: linux-arm64-vulkan
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
          - mise_env: linux-x64-egl
            runner: ubuntu-latest
          - mise_env: linux-x64-vulkan
            runner: ubuntu-latest
          - mise_env: macos-arm64-metal
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: macos-arm64-vulkan
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: macos-arm64-egl
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: ios-arm64-metal
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: ios-simulator-arm64-metal
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: windows-x64-vulkan
            runner: windows-2022
          - mise_env: windows-x64-wgl
            runner: windows-2022
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps

      - run: mise run configure
      - name: Build native library
        if: ${{ startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run build
      - name: Test native library
        if: ${{ !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run test

      - name: Build Java FFM binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/java-ffm:build
      - name: Build Java JNI binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/java-jni:build
      - name: Test .NET binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/dotnet:test
      # linux-arm64: no Kotlin/Native host — https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-36871
      # windows: no MSVC target — https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/SKIKO-446
      - name: Test Kotlin/Native binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'windows-') && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'linux-arm64-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/kotlin-native:build
      - name: Test Rust binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/rust:ci
      - name: Test Swift binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'macos-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/swift:test
      - name: Test Zig binding
        if: ${{ !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/zig:ci

      - name: Build LWJGL example
        if: ${{ contains(matrix.mise_env, 'vulkan') }}
        run: mise run //examples/lwjgl-map:build
      - name: Test Rust example
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //examples/rust-map:ci
      - name: Build Swift example
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env == 'macos-arm64-metal' }}
        run: mise run //examples/swift-map:build
      - name: Build Zig example
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //examples/zig-map:build
      - name: Run Zig readback example
        if: ${{ !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //examples/zig-readback:run

  required:
    name: ci-required
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 5
    if: ${{ always() }}
    needs:
      - hygiene
      - docs
      - variants
    steps:
      - name: Fail if required jobs failed
        if: ${{ contains(needs.*.result, 'failure') || contains(needs.*.result, 'cancelled') || contains(needs.*.result, 'skipped') }}
        run: |
          echo '${{ toJSON(needs) }}'
          exit 1

      - run: true

part.text:
Let me explore more of the repository structure to understand the conventions and tooling. I should look at:
1. The mise.toml to understand env definitions
2. The setup-ci-deps action
3. The scripts directory
4. The .miserc.toml
5. Any existing CI tasks in mise

Let me also understand the matrix mapping better. The matrix has envs and runners. Each step has conditions based on env prefixes.

Let me look at the existing tooling and conventions in scripts.
Now let me understand the mise env definitions and tooling conventions.

metadata.preview:
# https://mise.jdx.dev
# Mise is the contributor entrypoint: it pins tools, installs hooks, and runs
# repository tasks.

experimental_monorepo_root = true

[settings]
lockfile = true
lockfile_platforms = ["linux-x64", "linux-arm64", "macos-arm64", "windows-x64"]
pin = true
experimental = true
python.uv_venv_auto = "create|source"
windows_default_file_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; exec ""C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe"" ""$@""" _'
windows_default_inline_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; eval ""$1""" _'
unix_default_file_shell_args = "bash"
unix_default_inline_shell_args = "bash -e -lc"
# On Windows, the Microsoft Store Python alias in WindowsApps can appear before
# mise-managed Python. Keep mise tool paths first so file tasks run pinned tools.
activate_aggressive = true

display.text:
# https://mise.jdx.dev
# Mise is the contributor entrypoint: it pins tools, installs hooks, and runs
# repository tasks.

experimental_monorepo_root = true

[settings]
lockfile = true
lockfile_platforms = ["linux-x64", "linux-arm64", "macos-arm64", "windows-x64"]
pin = true
experimental = true
python.uv_venv_auto = "create|source"
windows_default_file_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; exec ""C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe"" ""$@""" _'
windows_default_inline_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; eval ""$1""" _'
unix_default_file_shell_args = "bash"
unix_default_inline_shell_args = "bash -e -lc"
# On Windows, the Microsoft Store Python alias in WindowsApps can appear before
# mise-managed Python. Keep mise tool paths first so file tasks run pinned tools.
activate_aggressive = true

[monorepo]
config_roots = [".devcontainer", "bindings/*", "examples/*", "docs"]

[tools]
# General tooling
"aqua:jdx/usage" = "3.3.0" # CLI completion for mise tasks
"aqua:jdx/hk" = "1.45.0" # Linter orchestration
"aqua:apple/pkl" = "0.31.0" # hk config dependency
"aqua:dprint/dprint" = "0.53.2" # Formatter orchestration
"github:sargunv/jvl" = "2026.05.03" # JSON validator
"aqua:rhysd/actionlint" = "1.7.8" # GH Actions linter
"aqua:koalaman/shellcheck" = "0.11.0" # Shell script linter (used by actionlint)

# C/C++ ecosystem
"github:prefix-dev/pixi" = "0.68.1"
"aqua:Kitware/CMake" = "4.3.3"
"aqua:ninja-build/ninja" = "1.13.2"
"conda:clang-format" = "22.1.7"
"conda:clang-tools" = "22.1.7"
"conda:doxygen" = "1.13.2"
"conda:glslang" = "16.3.0"
"conda:pkg-config" = "0.29.2"
"conda:vulkan-tools" = "1.4.341.0"
"github:cmakefmt/cmakefmt" = "1.3.0"
"aqua:XcodesOrg/xcodes" = { version = "1.6.2", os = ["macos"] }

# Node.js ecosystem
"core:node" = "24.11.0"
"aqua:pnpm/pnpm" = "10.33.2"

# Java/Kotlin ecosystem
"core:java" = "temurin-25.0.3+9.0.LTS"
"aqua:facebook/ktfmt" = "0.62"
"github:google/google-java-format" = "1.35.0"

# Python ecosystem
"core:python" = "3.14.4"
"aqua:astral-sh/uv" = "0.11.11"

# Swift ecosystem
"core:swift" = { version = "6.3.1", os = ["macos"] }
"github:nicklockwood/SwiftFormat" = "0.61.1"

# Other ecosystems
"core:rust" = { version = "1.95.0", profile = "minimal", components = "rustfmt,clippy" }
"core:zig" = "0.16.0"
"core:go" = "1.26.3"
"core:dotnet" = "10.0.203"

[env]
UV_PYTHON = { value = "{{ tools.python.path }}", tools = true }
MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT = "{{config_root}}"
MLN_FFI_VARIANT = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE = "{{env.MLN_FFI_CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE | default(value=\"RelWithDebInfo\")}}"
MLN_FFI_INCLUDE_DIR = "{{config_root}}/include"
MLN_FFI_VULKAN_INCLUDE_DIR = "{{config_root}}/third_party/maplibre-native/vendor/Vulkan-Headers/include"
MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_INCLUDE_DIR = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/include"
MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/lib"
MLN_FFI_ZIG_TARGET = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT = "{{env.MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT | default(value=\"30s\")}}"
CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default"
PKG_CONFIG_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/lib/pkgconfig"
LIBCLANG_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/lib"

[hooks]
postinstall = [
  { task = "sync-submodules" },
  "hk install --mise --quiet",
  "pixi install --locked --quiet",
  # conda-forge ships libclang-13.dll, but clang-sys on Windows searches for libclang.dll.
  # https://github.com/KyleMayes/clang-sys/issues/213
  # https://github.com/conda-forge/llvmdev-feedstock/issues/376
  '{% if os() == "windows" %}libclang_bin="$(dirname "$MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR")/bin"; cp "$libclang_bin/libclang-13.dll" "$libclang_bin/libclang.dll"{% endif %}',
  "uv sync --locked --quiet",
  "pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --loglevel warn",
  "dotnet tool restore --tool-manifest dotnet-tools.json --verbosity quiet",
  "dotnet tool restore --tool-manifest bindings/dotnet/dotnet-tools.json --verbosity quiet",
  # Astro type stubs for vp typeCheck on docs sources (.astro/ is gitignored).
  "pnpm --filter docs exec astro sync",
]

[tasks.configure]
depends = ["//:deps"]
run = [
  '''
  cmake_args=(
    -S .
    -B "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR"
    -G Ninja
    -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE="$MLN_FFI_CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE"
    -DMLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND="$MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND"
    -DMLN_FFI_OPENGL_CONTEXT_PROVIDER="$MLN_FFI_OPENGL_CONTEXT_PROVIDER"
    -DMLN_FFI_EGL_ROOT="$MLN_FFI_EGL_ROOT"
  )

  while IFS= read -r cmake_arg; do
    [[ -n "$cmake_arg" ]] && cmake_args+=("$cmake_arg")
  done <<< "${MLN_FFI_CMAKE_ARGS:-}"

  cmake "${cmake_args[@]}"
  ''',
  '''
  if [[ -f "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR/compile_commands.json" ]]; then
    mkdir -p build/clangd
    compile_commands_link="build/clangd/compile_commands.json"
    compile_commands_tmp="${compile_commands_link}.tmp"
    rm -f "$compile_commands_tmp"
    ln -s "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR/compile_commands.json" "$compile_commands_tmp"
    mv -f "$compile_commands_tmp" "$compile_commands_link"
  fi
  ''',
]

[tasks.build]
depends = ["//:configure"]
run = '''
build_args=(
  --build "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR"
  --parallel
)

cmake "${build_args[@]}"'''

[tasks.clean]
run = "rm -rf \"$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR\""

[tasks.deps]
description = "Install variant-specific managed dependencies."
run = '''
if [[ "$MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE" == "macos-arm64" ]]; then
  mise run deps:angle
fi
'''

[tasks.test]
depends = ["//:build"]
run = '''
(
  cd src/c_api/tests/zig
  zig build test {{ env.MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS }} --test-timeout "$MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT" --summary all --verbose
)
(
  cd bindings/zig
  zig build test {{ env.MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS }} --test-timeout "$MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT" --summary all --verbose
)
'''

[tasks.check]
run = "hk check --all"

[tasks.fix]
run = "hk fix --all"

[tasks.actions-up]
description = "Update and pin GitHub Actions steps."
run = "pnpm dlx actions-up"

[tasks._ktfmt]
description = "Called by dprint.jsonc"
hide = true
run = 'java -jar {{ tools["aqua:facebook/ktfmt"].path }}/ktfmt'
# ktfmt on windows doesn't work in git bash for some reason
shell = '{% if os() == "windows" %}cmd /c{% else %}sh -c -o errexit{% endif %}'

[tasks."ci:lint"]
run = [
  # Warm process-plugin cache before hk runs parallel dprint batches.
  "dprint output-resolved-config > /dev/null",
  "hk fix --all",
  "git update-index -q --refresh",
  # Lockfile drift is common across different mise builds
  "git diff --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'",
  "git diff --cached --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'",
]

[tasks."ci:vulkan-info"]
description = "Print the active Vulkan loader and driver summary."
run = "vulkaninfo --summary"

display.text:
name: Setup CI deps
description: Install sccache and mise toolchain

runs:
  using: composite
  steps:
    - name: Enable Git long paths
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Windows' }}
      shell: pwsh
      run: git config --global core.longpaths true

    - name: Cache MapLibre Native source
      uses: actions/cache@27d5ce7f107fe9357f9df03efb73ab90386fccae # v5.0.5
      with:
        path: |
          third_party/maplibre-native
          .git/modules/third_party/maplibre-native
        key: mln-src-v2-${{ hashFiles('.gitmodules', 'cmake/mln_version.cmake') }}

    - name: Install sccache
      uses: mozilla-actions/sccache-action@9e7fa8a12102821edf02ca5dbea1acd0f89a2696 # v0.0.10

    - name: Use Linux software graphics drivers
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Linux' }}
      shell: bash
      run: |
        sudo apt-get update
        sudo apt-get install --no-install-recommends --yes \
          libegl-mesa0 \
          libgl1-mesa-dri \
          libvulkan1 \
          mesa-utils \
          mesa-utils-extra \
          mesa-vulkan-drivers
        {
          echo "EGL_PLATFORM=surfaceless"
          echo "LIBGL_ALWAYS_SOFTWARE=true"
        } >> "$GITHUB_ENV"

    - name: Use Windows software graphics drivers
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Windows' }}
      shell: pwsh
      run: |
        $mesaVersion = "26.0.8"
        $mesaArchive = Join-Path $env:RUNNER_TEMP "mesa3d.7z"
        $mesaRoot = Join-Path $env:RUNNER_TEMP "mesa3d"
        $mesaUrl = "https://github.com/pal1000/mesa-dist-win/releases/download/$mesaVersion/mesa3d-$mesaVersion-release-msvc.7z"

        Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $mesaUrl -OutFile $mesaArchive
        $expectedHash = "A438C26C2752726916E455F9AD121F8A7E3CFECF8626251ABF8E5B3E129D8497"
        $actualHash = (Get-FileHash $mesaArchive -Algorithm SHA256).Hash
        if ($actualHash -ne $expectedHash) {
          throw "Mesa archive hash mismatch: expected $expectedHash, got $actualHash"
        }
        7z x $mesaArchive "-o$mesaRoot" -y | Out-Null

        $mesaWglDriver = Get-ChildItem $mesaRoot -Recurse -Filter "libgallium_wgl.dll" |
          Where-Object { $_.FullName -match "x64" } |
          Select-Object -First 1
        if (-not $mesaWglDriver) {
          throw "Missing Mesa WGL driver in $mesaArchive"
        }

        $driverManifest = Get-ChildItem $mesaRoot -Recurse -Filter "lvp_icd.x86_64.json" |
          Select-Object -First 1
        if (-not $driverManifest) {
          throw "Missing lavapipe Vulkan ICD manifest in $mesaArchive"
        }

        $mesaBin = $mesaWglDriver.Directory.FullName
        $mesaWglSystemDriver = Join-Path $env:WINDIR "System32\mesadrv.dll"
        Copy-Item $mesaWglDriver.FullName $mesaWglSystemDriver -Force

        $openglDriverKey = "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\OpenGLDrivers\MSOGL"
        New-Item -Path $openglDriverKey -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $openglDriverKey -Name DLL -PropertyType String -Value "mesadrv.dll" -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $openglDriverKey -Name DriverVersion -PropertyType DWord -Value 1 -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $openglDriverKey -Name Flags -PropertyType DWord -Value 1 -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $openglDriverKey -Name Version -PropertyType DWord -Value 2 -Force | Out-Null

        $driverKey = "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Khronos\Vulkan\Drivers"
        New-Item -Path $driverKey -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $driverKey -Name $driverManifest.FullName -PropertyType DWord -Value 0 -Force | Out-Null

        "GALLIUM_DRIVER=llvmpipe" >> $env:GITHUB_ENV
        "Path=$mesaBin;$env:Path" >> $env:GITHUB_ENV

    - name: Cache Cargo packages
      uses: actions/cache@27d5ce7f107fe9357f9df03efb73ab90386fccae # v5.0.5
      with:
        path: |
          ~/.cargo/registry/index/
          ~/.cargo/registry/cache/
          ~/.cargo/git/db/
        key: ${{ runner.os }}-${{ runner.arch }}-cargo-registry-v1-${{ hashFiles('**/Cargo.lock') }}

    - name: Cache Gradle
      uses: actions/cache@27d5ce7f107fe9357f9df03efb73ab90386fccae # v5.0.5
      with:
        path: |
          ~/.gradle/caches
          ~/.gradle/wrapper
        key: ${{ runner.os }}-${{ runner.arch }}-gradle-${{ hashFiles('**/*.gradle*', '**/gradle-wrapper.properties') }}
        restore-keys: |
          ${{ runner.os }}-${{ runner.arch }}-gradle-

    - name: Trim Windows PATH before mise activation
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Windows' }}
      shell: pwsh
      run: |
        $workspaceDrive = Split-Path -Qualifier $env:GITHUB_WORKSPACE
        # Git Bash maps /tmp to the checkout drive; create it before mise hooks run.
        New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path (Join-Path $workspaceDrive "tmp") | Out-Null

        $paths = @(
          if ($env:SCCACHE_PATH) { Split-Path -Parent $env:SCCACHE_PATH }
          if ($env:Path) { $env:Path -split ';' }
          "C:\Program Files\Git\bin",
          "C:\Program Files\Git\usr\bin",
          "$env:SystemRoot\System32",
          "$env:SystemRoot",
          "$env:SystemRoot\System32\Wbem",
          "$env:SystemRoot\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0",
          "C:\Program Files\PowerShell\7",
          "C:\Program Files\Git\cmd"
        ) | Where-Object { $_ -and (Test-Path $_) }

        "Path=$($paths -join ';')" >> $env:GITHUB_ENV

        $gradleTemp = $env:RUNNER_TEMP -replace '\\', '/'
        $gradleOptions = "-Djava.io.tmpdir=$gradleTemp"
        if ($env:GRADLE_OPTS) {
          $gradleOptions = "$gradleOptions $env:GRADLE_OPTS"
        }
        "GRADLE_OPTS=$gradleOptions" >> $env:GITHUB_ENV

    - name: Install mise toolchain
      uses: jdx/mise-action@1648a7812b9aeae629881980618f079932869151 # v4.0.1
      with:
        cache: true
        cache_key_prefix: mise-v2
        install: true

    - name: Wire sccache into CMake
      shell: bash
      run: |
        {
          echo "SCCACHE_GHA_ENABLED=true"
          echo "SCCACHE_GHA_VERSION=${MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE}"
          echo "SCCACHE_BASEDIRS=${GITHUB_WORKSPACE}"
          echo "CMAKE_C_COMPILER_LAUNCHER=sccache"
          echo "CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER_LAUNCHER=sccache"
        } >> "$GITHUB_ENV"

    - name: Diagnose EGL setup
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Linux' }}
      shell: bash
      run: |
        mise exec -- bash -euo pipefail -c '
          env | sort | grep -E "^(EGL|LIBGL|MESA|LD_LIBRARY_PATH|__EGL|MLN_FFI|VK_)" || true
          command -v eglinfo
          EGL_LOG_LEVEL=debug LIBGL_DEBUG=verbose LD_DEBUG=libs eglinfo || true
        '

    - name: Diagnose Vulkan setup
      if: ${{ contains(env.MISE_ENV, 'vulkan') }}
      shell: bash
      run: mise run ci:vulkan-info

    # The mise action may restore Rust metadata without all rustup-managed
    # component files. Repair the toolchain only when rustup cannot add the
    # configured components or the component binaries still cannot run.
    - name: Ensure Rust components
      shell: bash
      run: |
        ensure_rust_components() {
          mise exec -- rustup component add rustfmt clippy &&
            mise exec -- cargo fmt --version &&
            mise exec -- cargo clippy --version
        }

        if ! ensure_rust_components; then
          mise install --force rust
          ensure_rust_components
        fi

There are already per-variant config files in `.mise/config.<env>.toml`. Let me read them all to understand what data is available per variant.

1: [env]
2: MLN_FFI_VARIANT = "linux-x64-vulkan"
3: MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE = "linux-x64"
4: MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND = "vulkan"
5: MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR = "{{config_root}}/build/linux-x64-vulkan"
6: MLN_FFI_ZIG_TARGET = "x86_64-linux-gnu"
7: MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS = "-Dtarget=x86_64-linux-gnu -Dcmake-artifact-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR}}\" -Drender-backend=vulkan -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Dinclude-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_VULKAN_INCLUDE_DIR}}\" -Ddependency-library-dir=\"{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}\""
8: BINDGEN_EXTRA_CLANG_ARGS = "{% if os() == \"linux\" %}-isystem{{ exec(command='cc -print-file-name=include', cache_key='host-cc-builtin-include', cache_duration='1d') | trim }}{% endif %}"
9: CMAKE_LIBRARY_PATH = "{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}"
10: LIBRARY_PATH = "{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}"
11: LD_LIBRARY_PATH = "{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}}"
12: RUSTFLAGS = "-C link-arg=-Wl,-rpath,{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}} -C link-arg=-Wl,-rpath-link,{{env.MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR}} -C link-arg=-Wl,-rpath,{{env.MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR}}"

(End of file - total 12 lines)
</content>

- mise_env (the variant name)
- runner (the github runner)
- step conditions based on env patterns

I need to figure out what makes sense as a manifest. The variant config files in `.mise/config.<env>.toml` already define variants. The CI matrix adds:
- runner mapping
- step inclusion conditions

Let me look at all the variant config files and check if there's a CI-related structure already. Let me also check the AGENTS.md docs and the docs directory for context on conventions.

<content>
1: // hk orchestrates checks for pre-commit, `mise run check`, and `mise run fix`.
2: // Tools used here must be installed by `mise install`.
3: 
4: amends "package://github.com/jdx/hk/releases/download/v1.45.0/hk@1.45.0#/Config.pkl"
5: 
6: import "package://github.com/jdx/hk/releases/download/v1.45.0/hk@1.45.0#/Builtins.pkl"
7: 
8: local lintSteps = new Mapping<String, Step> {
9:   ["dprint"] = (Builtins.dprint) {
10:     // the default batch=false hits the cmd length limit on windows
11:     batch = true
12:   }
13:   ["actionlint"] {
14:     glob = List(".github/workflows/*.yml", ".github/workflows/*.yaml")
15:     check = "actionlint {{files}}"
16:   }
17:   ["jvl"] {
18:     glob = List("**/*.json", "**/*.jsonc")
19:     check = "jvl check {{files}}"
20:   }
21:   // Mirrors Builtins.`vp-check` (available in hk 1.46+). dprint owns formatting.
22:   ["vp-check"] {
23:     glob = List(
24:       "**/*.js",
25:       "**/*.mjs",
26:       "**/*.cjs",
27:       "**/*.ts",
28:       "**/*.mts",
29:       "**/*.cts",
30:       "**/*.jsx",
31:       "**/*.tsx",
32:       "**/*.vue",
33:       "**/*.svelte",
34:       "**/*.astro",
35:     )
36:     exclude = List("third_party/**")
37:     check = "pnpm exec vp check --no-fmt {{files}}"
38:     fix = "pnpm exec vp check --no-fmt --fix {{files}}"
39:   }
40:   ["ruff"] {
41:     glob = List("**/*.py")
42:     exclude = List("third_party/**")
43:     check = "uv run ruff check {{files}} && uv run ruff format --check {{files}}"
44:     fix = "uv run ruff check --fix {{files}} && uv run ruff format {{files}}"
45:   }
46:   ["ty"] {
47:     glob = List("**/*.py")
48:     exclude = List("third_party/**")
49:     check = "uv run ty check --error-on-warning .mise"
50:   }
51: }
52: 
53: hooks {
54:   ["pre-commit"] {
55:     fix = true
56:     stash = "git"
57:     steps {
58:       ...lintSteps
59:     }
60:   }
61:   ["check"] {
62:     steps {
63:       ...lintSteps
64:     }
65:   }
66:   ["fix"] {
67:     fix = true
68:     steps {
69:       ...lintSteps
70:     }
71:   }
72: }

(End of file - total 72 lines)
</content>

display.text:
name: Dev Container Image

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - .devcontainer/**
      - .mise/**
      - .miserc.toml
      - .github/workflows/devcontainer-image.yml
      - mise.lock
      - mise.toml
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - .devcontainer/**
      - .mise/**
      - .miserc.toml
      - .github/workflows/devcontainer-image.yml
      - mise.lock
      - mise.toml
  workflow_dispatch:

concurrency:
  group: devcontainer-image-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}

env:
  IMAGE: ghcr.io/maplibre/maplibre-native-ffi/devcontainer

jobs:
  build:
    name: build / ${{ matrix.arch }}
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request' || github.ref != 'refs/heads/main'
    permissions:
      contents: read
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.runner }}
    timeout-minutes: 90
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - arch: amd64
            runner: ubuntu-latest
          - arch: arm64
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - name: Install image tooling
        run: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install --no-install-recommends --yes skopeo
      - name: Build image
        run: mise run //.devcontainer:build --tag "maplibre-native-ffi-devcontainer:ci-${{ matrix.arch }}"

  release:
    name: release / ${{ matrix.arch }}
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name != 'pull_request'
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.runner }}
    timeout-minutes: 90
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - arch: amd64
            runner: ubuntu-latest
          - arch: arm64
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - name: Install image tooling
        run: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install --no-install-recommends --yes skopeo
      - name: Log in to GHCR
        run: echo "${{ github.token }}" | docker login ghcr.io --username "${{ github.actor }}" --password-stdin
      - name: Build and push image
        run: mise run //.devcontainer:build --tag "$IMAGE:main-${{ matrix.arch }}" --push

  manifest:
    name: manifest
    permissions:
      packages: write
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name != 'pull_request'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: release
    timeout-minutes: 10
    steps:
      - name: Log in to GHCR
        run: echo "${{ github.token }}" | docker login ghcr.io --username "${{ github.actor }}" --password-stdin
      - name: Publish multi-arch tag
        run: |
          docker buildx imagetools create \
            --tag "$IMAGE:main" \
            "$IMAGE:main-amd64" \
            "$IMAGE:main-arm64"

name: Docs Pages

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: read
  pages: write
  id-token: write

concurrency:
  group: pages
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:

    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 60
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3

name: Mirror to Codeberg

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    tags: ["**"]

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  mirror:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: codeberg
    timeout-minutes: 30
    steps:

display.text:
name: Mirror to Codeberg

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    tags: ["**"]

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  mirror:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: codeberg
    timeout-minutes: 30
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
        with:
          ref: main
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Push mirror
        env:
          CODEBERG_USERNAME: ${{ secrets.CODEBERG_USERNAME }}
          CODEBERG_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CODEBERG_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          {
            printf 'machine codeberg.org\nlogin %s\npassword %s\n' \
              "$CODEBERG_USERNAME" "$CODEBERG_TOKEN"
          } > ~/.netrc
          chmod 600 ~/.netrc

          git remote add mirror https://codeberg.org/maplibre/maplibre-native-ffi.git
          git fetch origin --tags
          git push mirror main
          git push mirror --tags

part.text:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me analyze the situation.

The CI matrix in `ci.yml` has these 11 variants:
1. linux-arm64-egl → ubuntu-24.04-arm
2. linux-arm64-vulkan → ubuntu-24.04-arm
3. linux-x64-egl → ubuntu-latest
4. linux-x64-vulkan → ubuntu-latest
5. macos-arm64-metal → macos-latest
6. macos-arm64-vulkan → macos-latest
7. macos-arm64-egl → macos-latest
8. ios-arm64-metal → macos-latest
9. ios-simulator-arm64-metal → macos-latest
10. windows-x64-vulkan → windows-2022
11. windows-x64-wgl → windows-2022

These map exactly to the `.mise/config.<env>.toml` files (all 11 of them exist). 

The CI also has a bunch of conditional steps. The conditions are based on:
- env name / prefix patterns
- The "config" the step is run for

The conditions are:
- Build native: `startsWith(env, 'ios-')`
- Test native: `!startsWith(env, 'ios-')`
- Build Java FFM: `env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(env, 'ios-')`
- Build Java JNI: same
- Test .NET: same
- Test Kotlin/Native: same + `!startsWith(env, 'windows-')` + `!startsWith(env, 'linux-arm64-')`
- Test Rust: same as java-ffm
- Test Swift: `env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && startsWith(env, 'macos-')`
- Test Zig: `!startsWith(env, 'ios-')`
- Build LWJGL example: `contains(env, 'vulkan')`
- Test Rust example: same as java-ffm
- Build Swift example: `env == 'macos-arm64-metal'`
- Build Zig example: same as java-ffm
- Run Zig readback example: `!startsWith(env, 'ios-')`

So we have 11 variants, and per variant, 14 step eligibility conditions. This is exactly the kind of thing that drifts.

Now, the policy question: what's the manifest, what's the generator, and what's the enforcement.

Key architectural decisions:

**Manifests**: Each variant already has a `.mise/config.<env>.toml` that defines build-related details. The CI matrix adds `runner` mapping. The natural approach is to add a CI manifest per variant OR a single CI manifest that references the variants.

Let me think about what makes sense. The cleanest design that:
1. Preserves current behavior unless clearly duplicated
2. Keeps normal contributor workflow simple (still `mise run test`, etc.)
3. Tests the generator
4. Stays in sync

I think the design should be:
- **Manifest**: A single source of truth describing CI target policy: which variants run in CI, on what runner, and which binding/example/native steps they run.
- **Generator**: A script (Python? The project uses Python for tooling with ruff/ty) that reads the manifest + the variant configs, and produces `.github/workflows/ci.yml`.
- **Enforcement**: A CI/lint check (part of `ci:lint` or a separate check) that regenerates the workflow and fails if it differs from checked-in.

But wait — there's a subtle decision. The existing `.mise/config.<env>.toml` files are *build* manifests. The CI matrix is *CI policy*. Should the CI runner mapping live in the `.mise/config.<env>.toml` files (so each variant is fully self-describing), or in a separate CI manifest?

Considerations:
- The mise configs are loaded at contributor build time. Adding GitHub-runner-specific data there mixes concerns (mise doesn't care about GitHub runners).
- But putting CI policy in a totally separate place means it can drift from the variant list (e.g., a variant added to `.mise/` but not added to CI).

Actually the cleanest approach: the **manifest should be the set of variant config files themselves** plus a small CI policy file that maps variants → runners and step capabilities. This way:
- Variants are the source of truth (you can't have CI for a variant that doesn't exist).
- The generator validates every CI variant has a config file and vice versa (no orphaned configs, no missing CI entries — well, that might be too strict, some variants may be contributor-only).

Hmm, but actually are there variants that exist but shouldn't be in CI? Looking at the list, all 11 configs are in CI. So a 1:1 mapping seems right, but I should allow opting out (e.g., a `ci = false` flag) in case a variant is contributor-only in the future. But "preserve current behavior" means all 11 are in CI now.

Let me reconsider the step conditions. Rather than encoding each step's eligibility as a condition expression (which is the drift-prone part), I think the cleaner model is:

**Capability-based**: Each variant declares what it supports (e.g., `supports_native_tests`, `supports_bindings`, `supports_kotlin_native`, etc.), and the generator produces the matrix with per-step `if:` conditions.

OR

**Step-list-based**: Each variant explicitly lists which CI steps it runs. More verbose but very explicit.

I think capability-based is the cleanest and most "declarative". Let me look at the conditions and derive the capability dimensions:

Looking at the conditions, they factor into these orthogonal capabilities:
1. `native_build` — always true (every variant builds)
2. `native_test` — true except ios-* (build-only targets)
3. `bindings` (java-ffm, java-jni, dotnet, rust, zig-map, rust-map) — true except macos-arm64-egl and ios-*
4. `kotlin_native` — bindings + not windows + not linux-arm64
5. `swift_binding` — macos-* (except macos-arm64-egl)
6. `zig_binding` — not ios-* (same as native_test actually!)
7. `lwjgl` — contains vulkan
8. `swift_example` — macos-arm64-metal only
9. `zig_readback` — not ios-* (same as native_test)

Wait, zig_binding condition is `!startsWith(env, 'ios-')` and native_test is also `!startsWith(env, 'ios-')`. They're identical. So zig binding runs wherever native test runs.

And zig_readback is also `!startsWith(env, 'ios-')`. So three steps share the same condition.

Hmm, this is getting complex. Let me think about whether to go capability-based or explicit per-step.

Actually, I realize the cleanest and least-drift-prone design is probably: **each variant lists exactly which steps it runs**, and the generator just translates that into matrix `if` conditions. This is maximally explicit and declarative. The downside is verbosity (11 variants × ~14 steps), but it's the most honest representation and the easiest to review.

But wait, that's a lot of redundancy. The capability model is more DRY. Let me think about what causes drift:
- Adding a new variant: capability model → add variant with capabilities; explicit model → add variant with full step list.
- Adding a new binding/step: capability model → add step + decide which capability gates it; explicit model → add step + decide per-variant.
- Changing a variant's support: capability model → toggle a capability; explicit model → toggle the step per variant.

The drift problem in the current YAML is that conditions are *implicit* (pattern-matched on env names). Making them *explicit* per-variant solves drift regardless of capability vs step-list.

I think I'll go with a hybrid that's actually clean: a **single CI policy manifest** (TOML, since the project loves TOML) that lists variants with their runner + a set of named "jobs" they run. The set of possible jobs is defined once (with their mise task), and each variant opts in. This is explicit per-variant but uses named jobs so adding a job is centralized.

Actually, let me reconsider. Let me reconsider what "declarative" and "manifests" (plural) suggest. The user said "generate the checked-in workflow from manifests". Plural "manifests" hints at multiple manifest files, possibly one per variant. The `.mise/config.<env>.toml` files are already per-variant manifests.

I think the most elegant design given the existing structure:
- Add a `[ci]` section (or separate `ci.toml`) co-located with each variant config in `.mise/config.<env>.toml`, OR
- A dedicated `.github/ci/` directory with per-variant manifests, OR
- A single `ci.toml` policy file.

Given the existing `.mise/config.<env>.toml` files are the variant manifests, and the user wants the workflow "generated from manifests", I lean towards **a single CI manifest** that's the policy, kept separate from build config (separation of concerns: CI runner choice is CI policy, not build policy). But it must reference the variants to stay in sync.

Hmm, but actually, thinking about it more — there's real value in co-locating. If I add `runner` and `ci` flags to each `.mise/config.<env>.toml`, then:
- Adding a variant means creating its config (already required) + setting CI fields. One place.
- The generator reads all configs, filters `ci.enabled = true`, emits matrix.
- Drift detection: a config without CI fields is just not in CI (could warn). 

But mixing GH-runner names into mise configs is a bit odd. However, mise configs already contain a lot of CI-relevant stuff (target triples, render backends). And the runner is conceptually "where this variant runs". I think it's defensible.

Actually, let me reconsider the cleanest separation. I'll ask myself: what would a maintainer most easily understand and edit?

Option A: Single `ci.toml` (or `.github/ci.toml`) listing all variants + runners + step membership.
Option B: Per-variant CI manifests (e.g., `.github/ci/<variant>.toml`).
Option C: Augment `.mise/config.<env>.toml` with CI fields.

I think Option A is cleanest for *CI policy* because:
- All CI policy in one file, easy to review the whole matrix at a glance.
- Clear separation from build config.
- Generator is simple: read one file, emit matrix.
- Sync enforcement: generator reads variants from `.mise/config.*.toml` to validate that CI variants ⊆ existing variants.

But Option B (per-variant manifests) better matches "manifests" plural and is more modular. With 11 variants it's a directory of small files.

Given the user explicitly said "manifests" (plural) and the project already has a per-variant config pattern in `.mise/`, I think per-variant CI manifests is the most consistent. But I don't want to over-engineer.

Let me reconsider: the most important drift vector is the *step conditions* (the messy `if:` expressions), not the variant list. The variant list (11 envs) is simple and rarely changes. The step conditions are what's "drifting" and "hand-written".

So the manifest's primary job is to **declare, per variant, which steps run**, and the generator turns that into the `if:` conditions (or better, into per-variant step lists).

Given all this, here's my decision. I'll go with:

**A single declarative manifest** at `.github/ci/variants.toml` (or similar) that:
- Lists each CI variant with: `mise_env`, `runner`, and the list of CI `steps` it runs (referencing named step definitions).
- Defines the catalog of `steps` once (name → mise task + display name).

**A generator** script (Python, since project uses Python+uv with ruff/ty for linting) at `scripts/generate_ci.py` (or `.github/ci/generate.py`) that reads the manifest and writes `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (the variants job portion).

**Enforcement**: 
- The generator is idempotent. 
- A check (added to `ci:lint` or a dedicated task) regenerates and diffs; fails if out of sync.
- Tests for the generator (pytest) since the user said "test the generator".

Wait, but if I regenerate the whole ci.yml, I need to preserve the non-variants parts (hygiene, docs, required jobs). Options:
1. Generator emits the entire ci.yml from a template + manifest.
2. Generator emits only the variants job, and there's a marker in ci.yml for where it goes.

Option 1 is cleaner for "stays in sync" — the whole file is generated. The template holds the static structure (triggers, hygiene/docs/required jobs, the variants job skeleton), and the manifest fills in the matrix + step conditions.

Hmm, but then the static structure (like checkout action SHAs) lives in a template. That's fine and actually good (templates are reviewable).

Actually, I realize I should think hard about the "preserve behavior" and "clearly duplicated policy" angles:

Looking at the conditions again, is there duplicated policy? 
- `bindings` group (java-ffm, java-jni, dotnet, rust, zig-map, rust-map) all share the EXACT same condition: `env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(env, 'ios-')`. That's duplicated policy 6 times.
- Three steps share `!startsWith(env, 'ios-')`: native_test, zig_binding, zig_readback.

So yes, there's clearly duplicated policy. A capability/group model would de-dup this.

So maybe the manifest should be **capability-based** after all, where:
- Variants declare capabilities.
- Steps declare which capability they require.
- Generator computes step eligibility per variant.

This de-dups the policy (the current duplication) and is declarative. Let me go with this — it directly addresses "clearly duplicated policy".

Let me define capabilities from the current conditions. Solve for a minimal set of predicates such that every step's condition = conjunction of capabilities.

Steps and their conditions:
1. native_build: always
2. native_test: !ios
3. java_ffm: !macos-egl && !ios
4. java_jni: !macos-egl && !ios
5. dotnet: !macos-egl && !ios
6. kotlin_native: !macos-egl && !ios && !windows && !linux-arm64
7. rust_binding: !macos-egl && !ios
8. swift_binding: !macos-egl && macos
9. zig_binding: !ios
10. lwjgl: contains vulkan
11. rust_example: !macos-egl && !ios
12. swift_example: macos-metal only
13. zig_example: !macos-egl && !ios
14. zig_readback: !ios

Capability dimensions (predicates over variant):
- A = !ios (i.e., can run device tests) 
- B = !macos-egl
- C = !windows
- D = !linux-arm64
- E = macos
- F = vulkan render backend
- G = macos-metal (specifically)

Hmm, this is getting complicated and somewhat arbitrary. The capability model requires choosing predicates, which is itself a design that could drift.

Let me step back and reconsider. Maybe the **explicit per-variant step list** is actually the most honest and least magical. The "duplication" in the current YAML isn't really harmful duplication — it's the same boolean expressed via env-name patterns, which is fragile. Expressing it as an explicit list per variant removes the fragility without introducing a capability-abstraction that needs maintenance.

Trade-off: explicit lists are verbose (each variant lists ~10-14 steps) but dead simple to read and change. Capability model is DRY but introduces indirection.

I'll go with **explicit per-variant step lists** using **named steps**. The named-steps catalog (step name → mise task + label) is defined once (de-duping the task definitions), and each variant lists the step names it runs. This:
- De-dups the task definitions (clear duplication).
- Makes per-variant policy explicit and reviewable.
- Removes the fragile env-name pattern matching.
- Is trivially declarative and easy to generate from.

This is the cleanest. Let me also decide: single manifest file vs per-variant files.

For 11 variants, a single TOML file with an array of tables is very readable and reviewable in one screen. Per-variant files would be 11 tiny files — more file churn, harder to see the whole matrix. I'll go with **a single manifest file** but structure it clearly. Actually, "manifests" plural in the prompt... I could interpret the named-steps catalog + the variants list as "manifests" (two sections). Or I could genuinely split: a `steps.toml` (catalog) and `variants.toml` (matrix). Hmm.

Actually, you know what, let me reconsider per-variant files once more, because it has a real sync benefit: the generator can enforce that **every** `.mise/config.<env>.toml` has a corresponding CI manifest (or is explicitly marked `ci = false`), preventing orphaned variants. With a single consolidated file, that cross-check is less natural (though still doable).

I think I'm overthinking the file layout. Let me make a decision and present it clearly. Given:
- The project already uses per-variant `.mise/config.<env>.toml`.
- The user said "manifests" (plural).
- Sync enforcement is a goal.

DECISION: I'll create **per-variant CI manifest files** under `.github/ci/variants/<env>.toml`, each declaring `runner` and the list of `steps` (by name). Plus a **steps catalog** at `.github/ci/steps.toml`. The generator reads all three sources (variant mise configs for validation, steps catalog, variant CI manifests) and emits `.github/workflows/ci.yml`.

Hmm, wait. Actually that's three locations and might be too much. Let me simplify.

Let me reconsider by thinking about who edits what:
- To add a variant: create `.mise/config.<env>.toml` (build) + `.github/ci/variants/<env>.toml` (CI) + run generator.
- To add a binding/step: add to `.github/ci/steps.toml` + add the step name to each variant that should run it + run generator.
- To change a variant's steps: edit its `.github/ci/variants/<env>.toml` + run generator.

That's reasonable. But the "add a step to each variant" is O(variants) edits. With capability model it'd be O(1). But explicitness has value.

Alternatively, single file `.github/ci.toml` with `[[variant]]` and `[steps]`:
- To add a variant: add a `[[variant]]` block.
- To add a step: add to `[steps.<name>]` + add name to relevant variants.
- One file, one edit location, full matrix visible.

I'll go with the **single manifest file** approach for simplicity and reviewability, but I'll cross-validate against `.mise/config.<env>.toml` for sync. This best satisfies "keep normal contributor workflow simple". The "manifests" plural can refer to the build manifests (`.mise/config.*.toml`) which ARE consulted by the generator + the CI manifest.

Hold on. Let me reconsider whether to merge CI fields INTO the `.mise/config.<env>.toml`. If I do that:
- One file per variant, fully self-describing.
- "manifests" = the per-variant config files (already the pattern).
- Generator reads `.mise/config.*.toml`, filters `ci.enabled`, emits workflow.
- Sync is natural: every variant config can have a `[ci]` table; the generator can require it (or default to enabled) — and can warn if a config lacks CI info.

This is actually the most elegant and matches the existing per-variant manifest pattern best. The only concern was "GH runner name in mise config is mixing concerns" — but I think `[ci]` as a clearly-delineated section is fine; mise ignores unknown sections. Let me verify mise ignores unknown top-level tables... mise config uses `[env]`, `[tasks]`, `[tools]`, `[hooks]`, `[settings]`, `[monorepo]`. Unknown tables — does mise error? I believe mise is lenient with unknown keys in config files (it just reads what it knows). Actually, I'm not 100% sure. Let me check by searching or reasoning. mise config schema — it might warn on unknown keys. To be safe, I could put CI metadata under `[env]` with MLN_FFI_CI_* prefixed keys, but that pollutes env. Or a dedicated file.

Hmm, the risk: mise might reject unknown `[ci]` table. Let me not risk it; I'll keep CI manifests separate from mise configs. 

Actually wait — I can test this empirically since mise is installed. But let me not go down that path; separation of concerns is cleaner anyway. CI policy ≠ build config.

OK final decision. Let me go with the cleanest design:

**Layout:**
- `.github/ci/manifest.toml` — single declarative CI manifest: `[steps]` catalog + `[[variant]]` matrix policy.
- `.github/ci/generate.py` — generator: reads manifest (+ validates variant names against `.mise/config.<env>.toml`), emits `.github/workflows/ci.yml`.
- `.github/ci/template.yml.j2` — Jinja2? No, avoid extra deps. Python string template. Or build YAML with a templating approach using stdlib + the existing structure.
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` — generated (checked in), with a header comment marking it generated.
- Tests: `tests/` or `.github/ci/test_generate.py` — pytest tests for the generator.
- A mise task `generate-ci` (or `ci:generate`) to run the generator.
- Enforcement: `ci:lint` (or a dedicated check) runs the generator and asserts no diff.

**Output approach:** The generator produces the ENTIRE ci.yml so it's fully reproducible. The static parts (name, on, permissions, concurrency, hygiene job, docs job, required job, and the variants job skeleton) come from the generator code (or an embedded template), and the dynamic parts (matrix.include + step list with conditions) come from the manifest.

For emitting YAML: I should produce clean, readable YAML matching the current style (2-space indent, etc.). I could use PyYAML but that reorders keys / uses its own formatting (not matching the hand-written style, and quotes handling differs). The project's `ci:lint` uses `dprint` which formats YAML via pretty_yaml wasm plugin. So I can emit *any* valid YAML and let dprint normalize formatting! That's a huge win — I don't need to match exact style; dprint will format it. And `ci:lint` already runs `hk fix` which runs dprint. So the checked-in file will be dprint-formatted.

But wait — if the generator emits non-dprint-formatted YAML, then the "regenerate and diff" enforcement would need to run dprint too, or the generator's raw output won't match the checked-in (dprint-formatted) file. So the generator should run dprint on its output before writing (or the enforcement runs dprint). Simplest: generator writes the file, then the enforcement step runs `hk fix` / `dprint fmt` on it, then diffs. Or the generator shells out to dprint. Let me have the generator produce formatted output by invoking dprint, OR just emit clean YAML manually with proper formatting.

Actually, hand-writing clean YAML output in Python via string building is very doable for this fixed structure and gives full control. PyYAML would mangle the GHA expression syntax (`${{ ... }}`) and ordering. So I'll build the YAML as strings, carefully formatted. Since the structure is fixed and small, this is fine and deterministic.

Let me reconsider: should I use a template engine? The project doesn't appear to use Jinja2 (no jinja in deps; Python deps are just ruff+ty). I'll avoid adding deps. String building in Python is fine. Or I can use Python's `string.Template` / f-strings with a clear structure.

Now, about the enforcement and "stays in sync":
1. **Generator idempotency**: running it twice produces identical output. (Tests verify.)

The capability model is declarative but still has a layer of inference (capability → step). Given "keeps drifting," the most robust against drift is the explicit per-variant list, because there's nothing to infer — what you see is exactly what runs. I'll go with explicit per-variant step lists. This also makes the generator trivially simple and the tests straightforward.

But to reduce the pain of "add a step to many variants," I can ALSO support a `default_steps` in the manifest that all variants inherit, plus per-variant additions/removals. Hmm, that reintroduces inference. No — keep it explicit. Adding a step is a deliberate act of choosing which variants run it; explicit listing forces that decision per variant, which is the point.

Actually, wait. Let me reconsider once more, because 11 variants × editing is genuinely annoying and could itself cause drift (forgetting to add a step to one variant). 

Compromise: support step *groups* in the catalog. A variant lists step names, which can include group names that expand to multiple steps. E.g., a group `bindings_desktop` = [java_ffm, java_jni, dotnet, kotlin_native, rust_binding, zig_binding]. No — that's the capability model in disguise and reintroduces the inference/drift.

I'll commit to **explicit per-variant step lists, no groups, no capabilities**. It's the clearest possible declaration. The generator validates every referenced step exists. Done.

Now, one more consideration: the user said "Preserve current CI behavior unless it is clearly duplicated policy." The current behavior is fully captured by the per-variant membership I derived. The "clearly duplicated policy" is the repeated `if` conditions — those are replaced by explicit membership (no duplication, since each variant states its own list). I'm preserving the *behavior* (same steps run on same variants) while eliminating the duplicated condition expressions. 

Now let me also think: should the generator emit `contains(matrix.steps, 'x')` or emit per-variant... no, matrix is shared. `contains(matrix.steps, 'x')` is the way. Let me verify GHA supports `contains()` on a matrix list value. `contains()` in GHA expressions works on strings and arrays? Actually, GHA `contains(search, item)` — when the first arg is a string, it's substring search; when it's an array, it checks membership. Wait, the signature is `contains(haystack, needle)`. If haystack is an array, needle membership. Hmm, actually I recall `contains` works on both strings and arrays. Let me verify: GitHub docs say `contains('Hello world', 'llo')` → true; `contains(fromJSON('["a","b"]'), 'a')` → hmm. Actually I've seen `contains(github.event.action, 'foo')` for strings. For arrays... I'm not 100% sure GHA `contains` supports array membership natively. Let me check.

From GitHub Actions expression docs: `contains(haystack, item)` — "Returns true if haystack contains item. ... If haystack is a string, returns true if item is a substring. ..." — actually I think it does support arrays too. But the matrix `steps` would be a list, and referencing `matrix.steps` — does GHA evaluate `contains(matrix.steps, 'x')` with array semantics? 

I recall there's ambiguity. To be safe, many people use `join(matrix.steps, ',')` and substring check, or store steps as a comma string. The most robust: store `steps` as a comma-separated string and use `contains(matrix.steps, 'x')` (substring). But then 'java' would match 'java_ffm'... no wait, substring 'java_ffm' in 'java_ffm,dotnet' is fine, but 'java' would also match. Since I use full step names as needles and they're comma-joined, a step name like 'dotnet' won't be a false substring of another. But 'build' is a substring of 'build_native' — and I don't have a bare 'build' step. To be safe with distinct names, comma-join and `contains(',' || matrix.steps || ',', ',' || 'step' || ',')` is bulletproof but ugly.

Hmm. Let me check whether GHA `contains` supports arrays. Actually, I'm now fairly confident GHA `contains` DOES support array membership. Evidence: the official docs example isn't there, but community usage `contains(matrix.something, 'x')` where matrix.something is a list works. Wait, let me reconsider. Actually I've definitely seen `contains(matrix.foo, 'bar')` fail because matrix.foo needs to be an array literal in the expression context.

Let me look at how GHA handles this. When you put `steps: ["a", "b"]` in matrix.include, then `matrix.steps` evaluates to an array. `contains(matrix.steps, 'a')`... 

I found conflicting memories. Let me just test it the robust way to avoid CI breakage: I'll have the generator verify against the *actual* checked-in ci.yml currently using string-prefix conditions — but I'm changing the mechanism. To be safe, I'll use the **string membership via comma-wrap** approach OR avoid `contains` on arrays entirely.

Actually, the cleanest robust approach that avoids the array-contains question: **don't use a steps list at all; use per-step boolean matrix keys.** Each variant in matrix.include has explicit `<step>: true/false` (or true omitted = false). Then `if: matrix.<step>`. This is unambiguous, well-supported, and very readable. The matrix.include entries get wider, but each is a clear declaration.

Wait, but that's 14 booleans × 11 variants = wide. Alternatively, only include `true` keys (omit false ones) and use `if: matrix.java_ffm == true`? But referencing an undefined matrix key errors. Hmm. With `matrix.foo` where some entries lack `foo`, GHA evaluates `matrix.foo` to empty/null for those? Actually no — matrix keys must be consistent or you get null. `if: matrix.foo` with matrix.foo being null/undefined → I think GHA treats missing matrix property as empty string, and `if:` on empty string is falsy → step skipped. But referencing a truly undefined matrix key may error. With `matrix.include`, extra keys per entry are allowed and merged; entries without the key get it as... null? Let me think.

This is getting into GHA semantics weeds. The SAFEST, most battle-tested approach: keep `steps` as a **string** (space or comma separated) and use `if: contains(matrix.steps, 'stepname')`. String `contains` is 100% supported (substring). To avoid false substring matches, ensure no step name is a substring of another OR pad. My step names: build_native, test_native, java_ffm, java_jni, dotnet, kotlin_native, rust_binding, swift_binding, zig_binding, lwjgl, rust_example, swift_example, zig_example, zig_readback. Substring risks: 'java_ffm' vs 'java_jni' — distinct. 'rust_binding' vs 'rust_example' — distinct (binding vs example). 'zig_binding' vs 'zig_example' vs 'zig_readback' — distinct. 'swift_binding' vs 'swift_example' — distinct. 'build_native' vs 'test_native' — distinct. 'dotnet' unique. 'lwjgl' unique. 'kotlin_native' — contains 'native'? 'native' substring is in build_native, test_native, kotlin_native. But I never search for 'native' alone. I search for full names. So full-name substring search is safe as long as no full name is a substring of a *concatenation* of others. With space-separation, 'kotlin_native' won't be a false substring of any space-joined list unless it's actually present. And 'java' isn't a needle. So full names as needles in a space/comma-joined haystack is safe. 

But to be extra safe and clear, I'll use **comma-separated with comma-wrap in the contains**: `if: contains(format(',{0},', matrix.steps), ',java_ffm,')`. Hmm, `format` works. Or simpler: store steps as a comma-joined string in the manifest/matrix and use `contains(matrix.steps, 'java_ffm')` relying on name uniqueness. Given name uniqueness analysis above, plain `contains(matrix.steps, 'java_ffm')` on a comma/space string is safe.

Wait, actually, let me reconsider — I'd really prefer the cleanest. Let me check definitively whether GHA `contains` supports array membership, because if it does, `steps: [...]` (array) + `contains(matrix.steps, 'x')` is the cleanest.

Let me look it up via webfetch to be certain, since this is consequential (CI breakage).

Actually, I recall now with high confidence: **GitHub Actions `contains()` does support arrays.** The docs say: "contains(haystack, item) Returns true if haystack contains item as a substring when haystack is a string, or as an element when haystack is an array." Hmm, I'm not sure the docs say the array part. Let me just verify with webfetch to be safe. This is worth getting right.

Let me also reconsider — maybe avoid the whole question. Alternative mechanism: **generate per-step `if` conditions as explicit boolean expressions over `matrix.mise_env`**, recomputed from the manifest. I.e., the manifest declares per-variant membership, and the generator synthesizes the old-style `if: matrix.mise_env != '...' && ...` conditions by computing, for each step, the set of variants that include it and emitting an equivalent condition. But that's reintroducing the complex hand-written conditions (just generated). It works and is robust (no contains/array question), but the conditions become long OR-of-equality or re-derived AND expressions. E.g., for swift_example (only macos-arm64-metal): `if: matrix.mise_env == 'macos-arm64-metal'`. For java_ffm (many variants): could express as `if: matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-')` — but that requires the generator to rediscover the pattern, which is fragile/complex. OR emit explicit: `if: contains(format('...'), matrix.mise_env)` listing the variants. E.g. `if: contains('linux-arm64-egl linux-arm64-vulkan ...', matrix.mise_env)`. That's a generated, always-correct condition derived directly from membership. This avoids arrays entirely and is robust!

So: for each step, the generator computes the sorted list of variants that include it, and emits `if: contains('<space-joined variant list>', matrix.mise_env)`. This is a string substring check on mise_env (a scalar), 100% supported, and derived directly from the manifest. No arrays, no contains-on-array ambiguity. And it's declarative — the manifest is the source, the condition is generated.

But wait, substring risk on mise_env names: 'linux-x64-egl' vs 'linux-x64-vulkan' — searching for 'linux-x64-egl' in a space-joined string is fine (space-delimited). 'linux-arm64-egl' contains 'linux-arm64' which is substring of 'linux-arm64-egl' and 'linux-arm64-vulkan', but the needle is the full env name with spaces around, so 'linux-arm64-egl' only matches itself. Since needles are full env names and haystack is space-joined, and I search contains(haystack, needle) where needle is a full env name, the only risk is if one env name is a substring of the concatenation spanning a boundary — e.g., 'a b' where 'a'+'b'... but env names don't contain spaces, and the needle is a full name; a false match would require the needle to appear spanning two space-separated names, which needs a space in the needle — none. And within a single name, the needle equals a full name only if that name is present. So safe. But to be bulletproof, wrap with spaces: `contains(format(' {0} ', matrix.<list>), format(' {0} ', matrix.mise_env))` — eh, overkill. Plain space-join with leading/trailing space is enough. Actually `contains` takes (haystack, needle); I'll do haystack = ' ' + join(' ') + ' ', needle = ' ' + mise_env + ' '. Bulletproof. But that's verbose in YAML.

Hmm, this is also getting ugly. Let me step back and just verify GHA array-contains, because if it works, the `matrix.steps` array + `contains(matrix.steps, 'x')` is by far the cleanest and most readable, and it's the natural fit.

Let me webfetch the GHA expressions reference.

Actually, I'm now quite confident from memory that GHA added array support to `contains`. Multiple production workflows use `contains(matrix.foo, 'bar')` with `matrix.foo` being a list. And the `fromJSON` pattern is used precisely because matrix context values are sometimes strings unless fromJSON'd — but matrix.include array values ARE arrays. I'll verify with a quick webfetch to be safe, then proceed.

Let me also reconsider: even simpler and unambiguous — use `fromJSON`? No. Let me just verify contains-array.

Let me do the webfetch and also explore the docs folder for any existing CI/generator conventions, and check if there's a tests directory convention.

Let me proceed with research in parallel.
I have a clear picture of the build manifests. Now let me verify GitHub Actions `contains()` array semantics (consequential for the generator design) and check the docs/testing conventions.

display.text:
---
title: Overview
description: Contributor setup, project scope, workflow commands, tests, and examples.
sidebar:
  order: 1
---

## Project Scope

The project exposes MapLibre Native through two layers.

The C API exposes core MapLibre Native features on supported native platforms:
runtime, resources, maps, cameras, events, diagnostics, logging, render target
primitives, texture readback, and low-level extension points such as resource
providers and URL transforms. It excludes convenience APIs such as snapshotting
and platform integrations such as gestures and device sensors.

Language bindings sit directly above the C API. In the target language, they
manage C handles, struct initialization, scoped lifetimes, status codes,
diagnostics, borrowed data, threading, and event draining. They preserve the C
API's concepts. Higher-level adapters may provide full SDKs, async models, view
lifecycle integrations, convenience workflows, or new abstractions.

Read the
[Binding specification](/maplibre-native-ffi/development/binding-specification/)
before implementing or reviewing a binding.

## Getting Set Up

Install the platform toolchain:

- On macOS, install the Xcode version listed in `.xcode-version`, or a recent
  compatible Xcode. Run the pinned
  [`xcodes`](https://github.com/XcodesOrg/xcodes) tool with `xcodes select` to
  switch to the repository version.
- On Fedora, install the `C Development Tools and Libraries` package group with
  `sudo dnf group install c-development`.
- On Debian or Ubuntu, install the C and C++ compiler packages with
  `sudo apt install build-essential`.
- On Windows, install a recent version of Visual Studio Community (or Build
  Tools) 2022 with the `Desktop development with C++` workload and
  `Git for Windows`. We rely on Git Bash to run project scripts.

Install [`mise`](https://mise.jdx.dev/), then install pinned project tools and
run repository setup hooks:

```bash
mise install
```

The setup hooks install project dependencies and initialize the MapLibre Native
submodule at `third_party/maplibre-native`.

Run the Zig map example as a smoke test:

```bash
mise run //examples/zig-map:run
```

Mise selects the native build profile that matches your host when one is
available. Set `MISE_ENV=<variant>` before `mise run ...`, or pass
`mise -E <variant> run ...`, to build, test, or run examples for another
platform and render backend.

## Common Commands

```bash
# Build and test the C API (via Zig bindings)
mise run test

# Build only
mise run build

# Run linters and formatters
mise run fix

# Run examples
mise run //examples/zig-map:run

# Build the documentation site
mise run //docs:build
```

## How Tools Fit Together

This repository spans native code, language bindings, examples, tests, and
documentation. Each tool owns the layer where it has the clearest dependency
model.

Project-managed dependency state keeps builds reproducible. Mise supplies
repository tools, owns task execution, and exports the environment consumed by
build systems, bindings, and examples. Pixi currently supplies desktop native
libraries from [`conda-forge`](https://conda-forge.org/). Platform SDKs such as
Xcode, Visual Studio, Linux compiler packages, and the Android SDK remain host
toolchain inputs.

[`mise`](https://mise.jdx.dev/) is the contributor entrypoint. It pins top-level
tools, installs Git hooks, selects backend variants, and runs repository tasks.
Use `mise run ...` for common workflows: build, test, check, fix, and examples.
Mise also provides ecosystem entrypoints such as Pixi, Zig, Python, uv, Node,
and pnpm.

[`pixi`](https://pixi.sh/) installs the desktop native libraries used by local
builds. CMake, Ninja, pkg-config, shader tools, Doxygen, clang-format, and
clang-tidy are pinned by mise. Host platform toolchains provide C and C++
compilers, and CMake builds the native C/C++ library.

Language package managers own dependencies inside their ecosystems. For example,
`uv` owns Python package dependencies, `pnpm` owns Node package dependencies,
Gradle owns Java and Kotlin dependencies, and Cargo owns Rust dependencies.
Language-specific formatters, linters, analyzers, test frameworks, and code
generators usually live with the language package graph they serve.

[`hk`](https://github.com/jdx/hk) orchestrates repository checks for pre-commit,
`mise run check`, and `mise run fix`. [`dprint`](https://dprint.dev/) owns
repository-wide formatting defaults.

[Astro](https://astro.build/) and [Starlight](https://starlight.astro.build/)
build the documentation site. Generated API reference HTML is installed into
`docs/public/reference/` before each docs build.

## Tests And Examples

Every feature needs automated CI coverage when practical. The root
`mise run test` command builds the native library, runs the direct Zig C API
suite, and runs the public Zig binding suite for supported host variants.

Use examples for demos and behavior that needs manual validation, such as visual
output, interactive input, or host graphics integration.

Keep examples small. This repository includes low-level language bindings and
focused integration examples. Full application SDKs live outside this
repository.

The GitHub Actions documentation for `contains` states: "contains(haystack, item) ... If haystack is a list/array, returns true if item is an element of the array." This is well-established. Many production workflows use `contains(matrix.tags, 'foo')` with matrix arrays. I'll proceed with the array approach but ALSO make the design robust by... no, I'll commit to array contains. It's correct.

Actually, to be safe against my own uncertainty and avoid CI breakage that I can't test here, let me choose the MOST robust representation that doesn't depend on array-contains semantics. The scalar-string approach on `matrix.mise_env` is bulletproof:

For each gated step, generate `if: contains('<variant-list>', matrix.mise_env)` where `<variant-list>` is a fixed string of the variants that run that step. This uses string `contains` on a scalar — 100% supported, unambiguous. But substring safety: I'll join with a delimiter that ensures no false matches. Using a space-separated list and full env-name needles is safe (env names have no spaces; a needle only matches if the exact env name appears delimited). To be fully bulletproof, I'll wrap: haystack = " env1 env2 ... " (spaces around), needle = " env ". Actually since I'm generating, I can make it `contains(' env1 env2 ', format(' {0} ', matrix.mise_env))` — wait `contains(haystack, needle)`. So haystack is the constant list-string, needle is the mise_env. I'd write `if: contains('linux-x64-egl macos-arm64-metal ', format('{0} ', matrix.mise_env))` — hmm, trailing space per item. Let me just do: list string = ' ' + ' '.join(variants) + ' ', and needle = ' ' + mise_env + ' ' via format. Then `contains(' linux-x64-egl macos-arm64-metal ', format(' {0} ', matrix.mise_env))`. This guarantees whole-token matching. But it's verbose/ugly in the YAML.

Hmm. The `matrix.steps` array + `contains(matrix.steps, 'x')` is MUCH cleaner. And I'm confident it works. Let me go with it. If I'm wrong about array support, CI would catch it on the first run and it's a one-line generator change. But I'm not wrong.

Actually — you know what, let me make a final robust decision that's clean AND unambiguous: use the array approach `contains(matrix.steps, 'step_name')`. This is clean, readable, declarative, and correct. Confirmed by GHA docs. Moving on.

Wait, but actually there's an even cleaner consideration. Let me reconsider the whole emission strategy to minimize `if:` noise and maximize clarity.

With `matrix.steps` as an array per variant and `if: contains(matrix.steps, 'x')`, the generated variants job looks like:

```yaml
  variants:
    name: variant / ${{ matrix.mise_env }}
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.runner }}
    timeout-minutes: 120
    env:
      MISE_ENV: ${{ matrix.mise_env }}
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - mise_env: linux-arm64-egl
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
            steps: [test_native, java_ffm, java_jni, dotnet, rust_binding, zig_binding, rust_example, zig_example, zig_readback]
          ...
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@<sha> # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - run: mise run configure
      - name: Build native library
        if: ${{ contains(matrix.steps, 'build_native') }}
        run: mise run build
      - name: Test native library
        if: ${{ contains(matrix.steps, 'test_native') }}
        run: mise run test
      ...
```

That's clean and the policy is fully visible in the matrix.include.

Is formatted such that dprint (pretty_yaml) is a no-op (so ci:lint's "regenerate + git diff" stays clean), OR the workflow tolerates dprint reformatting (if dprint reformats, then the checked-in file = dprint-formatted generator output, and regeneration produces non-dprint-formatted → diff → but ci:generate runs before git diff in ci:lint, and after hk fix... wait order matters).

Let me re-examine ci:lint ordering concern. ci:lint:
1. dprint output-resolved-config (warm cache)
2. hk fix --all (runs dprint + other formatters, writes changes)
3. (NEW) mise run ci:generate (regenerates ci.yml from manifest)
4. git update-index --refresh
5. git diff --exit-code (fails if any unstaged changes)
6. git diff --cached --exit-code

If step 3 (ci:generate) writes ci.yml in non-dprint format, then step 5 sees a diff (the just-regenerated ci.yml differs from committed) → FAILS. That's the sync check working: it tells you "ci.yml doesn't match the manifest." But wait — the committed ci.yml IS generated from the manifest (by me). So after regeneration, ci.yml should match the committed version byte-for-byte. The question is whether the committed version is dprint-formatted and whether regeneration produces dprint-formatted output.

Scenario: I generate ci.yml with the generator (some format), then run dprint on it (via hk fix) which reformats it to dprint style, then commit. Now committed = dprint style. Later, in ci:lint, step 2 (hk fix) runs dprint on committed → no change (already dprint style). Step 3 (ci:generate) regenerates → produces GENERATOR-RAW format (not dprint style) → overwrites ci.yml with raw format. Step 5 git diff → ci.yml now differs from committed (raw vs dprint) → FAILS. BAD. This would make ci:lint always fail.

So the generator's output MUST be byte-identical to what dprint produces on it. Two ways:
(A) Generator outputs dprint-canonical format (I hand-match dprint's style exactly).
(B) Generator invokes dprint on its output before writing.

(B) is robust but couples generator to dprint (and dprint must be on PATH; in tests it'd need dprint). For hermetic tests, (A) is better but fragile to dprint version changes.

(C) Best: the generator outputs its OWN canonical format, and the "sync check" regenerates to a TEMP file and diffs (without dprint in the loop), AND the committed file is committed in the generator's format (NOT run through dprint). - dprint ignores ci.yml.
- ci:lint: hk fix (skips ci.yml), ci:generate (regenerates ci.yml in generator format), git diff (ci.yml must match committed generator format) → enforces sync.
- The committed ci.yml is in generator format (committed once after running generator).

This is clean! Exclude the generated file from dprint (it's generated, formatting is owned by the generator), and the generator owns its format. The sync check is simply "regenerate and diff." No dprint coupling, hermetic tests. 

But wait — does excluding ci.yml from dprint conflict with actionlint? actionlint still lints it (good, actionlint isn't dprint). actionlint is run by hk on `.github/workflows/*.yml`. Running ci:generate inside ci:lint means ci:lint modifies ci.yml if out of sync, then git diff fails — which is the desired enforcement (it both reports and would-be-fixes, but fails to force the contributor to regenerate+commit). Locally, `mise run ci:lint` would fix it and then fail the diff, prompting commit. That's slightly annoying (it fixes then complains). Better approach for the check: generate to a temp file and diff against committed, fail if different, WITHOUT overwriting. Then ci:lint is purely diagnostic.
